Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.

You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 22 of about 48 target actions so far.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 11 actions returned the board to a state already seen 11 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 11 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Good, recovered from game over. The board is back to 14 green blocks.
Pattern so far:
- Collecting top row (7 blocks) works
- Collecting bottom row triggers game over
Maybe the mechanic is:
1. Collect all top row blocks
2. Move agent to trigger phase change (like in level 1 where moving right triggered pink cross)
3. Then collect bottom row blocks
4. Move agent again to complete
In level 1, after collecting all green blocks, moving the agent right triggered the pink cross phase. Then moving left to the pink cross column completed the level.
For level 2, maybe:
1. Collect all 7 top row blocks
2. Move agent right (to trigger something)
3. Collect all 7 bottom row blocks
4. Move agent left (to complete)
Let me try this hypothesis. First collect all 7 top row blocks, then move right, then collect bottom row.
Top row centers: (27, 15), (27, 21), (27, 27), (27, 33), (27, 39), (27, 45), (27, 51)

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: Recovered. Hypothesis: Need to collect top row, move agent right (trigger phase), collect bottom row, move left (complete). Let me test:

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Collect all 7 top row blocks one at a time
top_row = [(27, 15), (27, 21), (27, 27), (27, 33), (27, 39), (27, 45), (27, 51)]

for i, (r, c) in enumerate(top_row):
    result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': r, 'col': c}])
    if result.get('game_over', False):
        print(f"Game over after top row click {i+1} at ({r}, {c})")
        break
    if result.get('level_completed', False):
        print(f"Level completed after top row click {i+1} at ({r}, {c})")
        break

# Check state
seg = current_frame.segmentation
from collections import Counter
colors = Counter(n['color'] for n in seg['nodes'])
print(f"\nAfter top row: colors={dict(colors)}")
green = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
print(f"Green blocks: {len(green)}")

# Now move agent right
result = action(['RIGHT'])
print(f"\nAfter RIGHT: game_over={result.get('game_over', False)}, level_completed={result.get('level_completed', False)}")

seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
colors2 = Counter(n['color'] for n in seg2['nodes'])
print(f"After RIGHT colors: {dict(colors2)}")
green2 = [n for n in seg2['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
print(f"Green blocks: {len(green2)}")

# Check for new objects (like pink cross in level 1)
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'R' or n['color'] == 'M' or n['color'] == 'P':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  New object: color={n['color']}, rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
